examples.title: Generic PRNG Algorithm Structure
examples.description: Pseudocode showing the general structure of a PRNG with seeding, state transition, and output generation phases
examples.code:
// Generic PRNG Algorithm Structure
BEGIN PRNG_Algorithm
 // Phase 1: Seeding
 FUNCTION Initialize(seed)
 state ← seed
 RETURN state
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Phase 2: State Transition
 FUNCTION Transition(currentState)
 // Mathematical function to compute next state
 nextState ← f(currentState)
 RETURN nextState
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Phase 3: Output Generation
 FUNCTION Output(currentState)
 // Extract pseudorandom bits from state
 output ← g(currentState)
 RETURN output
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Main PRNG Operation
 FUNCTION Generate( seed, numValues )
 state ← Initialize(seed)
 outputSequence ← []
 
 FOR i ← 1 TO numValues DO
 output ← Output(state)
 outputSequence.append(output)
 state ← Transition(state)
 END FOR
 
 RETURN outputSequence
 END FUNCTION
END PRNG_Algorithm
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the three main phases of any PRNG: initialization from seed, state transition to next state, and output generation. The functions f() and g() represent the transition and output functions that define specific PRNG algorithms.

examples.title: PRNG with Reseeding for Forward Security
examples.description: Enhanced PRNG showing how reseeding injects additional entropy to provide forward security
examples.code:
// PRNG with Reseeding Mechanism
BEGIN SecurePRNG
 VARIABLES:
 state // Internal state
 entropyPool // Buffer for additional entropy
 
 // Initial Seeding
 FUNCTION Initialize(seed, initialEntropy)
 state ← Mix(seed, initialEntropy)
 entropyPool ← []
 RETURN state
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Mixing function combines inputs
 FUNCTION Mix(input1, input2)
 // Cryptographic mixing (e.g., hash-based)
 result ← Hash(input1 || input2)
 RETURN result
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Generate with optional reseeding
 FUNCTION GenerateWithReseed(currentState, numValues, additionalEntropy)
 state ← currentState
 outputSequence ← []
 
 // Inject new entropy if provided
 IF additionalEntropy ≠ NULL THEN
 state ← Mix(state, additionalEntropy)
 END IF
 
 FOR i ← 1 TO numValues DO
 // Generate output
 output ← Extract(state)
 outputSequence.append(output)
 
 // Update state with transition
 state ← Transition(state)
 
 // Periodic reseeding for forward security
 IF i mod ReseedInterval == 0 THEN
 state ← Mix(state, FreshEntropy())
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 RETURN outputSequence, state
 END FUNCTION
END SecurePRNG
examples.explanation: This algorithm shows reseeding which provides forward security - even if current state is compromised, attacker cannot predict past outputs. Fresh entropy is periodically mixed into state to ensure forward security.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) with periodic overhead for mixing

examples.title: State Compromise Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates forward and backward security properties by showing what information is exposed/leaked in different compromise scenarios
examples.explanation: This pseudocode illustrates what forward and backward security mean in practice. Forward security ensures past outputs remain secret even if current state is compromised. Backward security ensures the original seed cannot be determined from current state.
examples.timeComplexity: Varies by operation (hash functions typically O(n))
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is output history stored
